intro_band.agent_remit_summary: CraftBot is declared as a self-hosted, general-purpose personal AI agent that works alongside a single local operator like a remote employee — executing tasks, maintaining a local memory of the operator's preferences, proactively planning on a schedule, and building and operating its own local "Living UI" applications. It may use file operations, web research, document generation, memory search, task scheduling, operator-connected integrations (Google Workspace, Slack, Notion, Telegram, GitHub, Stripe, and similar), and operator-installed skills and MCP servers, acting only on the operator's behalf using the operator's own credentials. Arbitrary shell/code execution and running imported third-party code MUST run isolated from the host or require operator approval and MUST NOT silently inherit the operator's full secret-bearing environment; irreversible or externally-visible actions (sends, posts, payments, third-party writes) require code-enforced human approval; and its control surface must never be exposed to a network-reachable, unauthenticated caller.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python application (self-hosted desktop/service) orchestrated through a large app/agent_base.py with a pluggable action framework under agent_core/core and app/data/action. Model-generated action code is run by agent_core/core/impl/action/executor.py — either in-process via exec() or in a persistent venv subprocess that provides no OS isolation — and a first-class run_shell action shells out directly. The operator interface is an aiohttp WebSocket control plane in app/ui_layer/adapters/browser_adapter.py (localhost:7926); a FastAPI Living UI sidecar proxy and generated Living UI backends bind 0.0.0.0 with wildcard CORS. Credentials are plaintext JSON under .credentials/; memory is a chromadb vector store plus editable agent_file_system/ identity files; 157 MCP servers ship in app/config/mcp_config.json. An app/security/ package with a prompt sanitizer and secure error handler exists but is never imported.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is a rich framework whose declared safety envelope lives almost entirely in prompt text and dead code rather than in the execution path. CraftBot runs model-chosen shell commands (run_shell) and model-authored Python (executor.py) with the operator's full environment, no isolation, and no code-enforced approval gate — the "sandbox" is a bare venv and the bundled app/security sanitizer/error-handler are never wired in — while untrusted web and message content flows unfiltered into context and an autonomous 30-minute proactive heartbeat can reach those execution surfaces. The single most important takeaway is a complete external-content → autonomous-loop → code-execution chain with no interposition, compounded by a writable identity/memory store (editable SOUL.md/AGENT.md and a self-written chromadb memory) that turns one injection into cross-session persistence. Real but partial controls exist around the edges — a wired LLM rate limiter, an idempotency/activity ledger for irreversible actions, credentials chmod'd and git-ignored, rotating logs — but none of them constrain the core execution model.

rules.rule_text: It must act solely on behalf of, and under the control of, the operator who runs it — never as an autonomous party acting against the operator's interest, and never as a service exposed to untrusted third parties.
Exposing its control surface (task execution, settings, files, credentials) to the public internet or to other users.
Inbound messages from a messaging channel must never be treated as authenticated operator commands without a sender-identity check.
The primary operator interface; must be reachable only from the local machine.
Sending mail or posting publicly is a high-impact outbound action.

Because this capability can read, modify, or destroy any file the operator can and can reach the network, it MUST run isolated from the host or require operator approval before executing a state-changing or destructive command, and it MUST NOT silently inherit the operator's full secret-bearing environment.
These MUST be isolated from the operator's credentials and broader filesystem and MUST come from a source the operator approved.
Any capability that exposes agent control, files, or credentials to a network-reachable, unauthenticated caller.
Credentials or secrets MUST NOT be written to chat, logs, or any world-readable or version-controlled file.
Secrets MUST be stored protected at rest (OS keychain or encrypted store), not as plaintext files, and MUST be excluded from any data the agent transmits.

Any irreversible or externally-visible action: sending email/messages, posting publicly, making payments or other financial operations, and creating/modifying/deleting data in a connected third-party account.
an agent-initiated high-impact action requires explicit operator approval, and this gating MUST be enforced by code, not left to model discretion alone.
Fabricating success for an action that failed.
Editing the operator-owned identity/policy files (SOUL.md, USER.md, AGENT.md) or the long-term memory store without operator confirmation.
Any attempt by external/untrusted content to drive a high-impact action.
Scheduled jobs / cron tasks: as defined in the scheduler; no undeclared recurring outbound activity.
Maximum retries before escalation: one retry for a transient error; then stop and escalate.

findings.summary: The run_shell action executes model-chosen commands via subprocess with the operator's full environment, no approval gate, and no isolation.
findings.description: run_shell builds the process environment from os.environ.copy() and runs the model-supplied command string directly (cmd/powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass / system shell), so any command the model emits runs with the operator's full secret-bearing environment and file/network access. There is no allow/deny list, no per-command policy, and no code-enforced confirmation. This is precisely the remit's Example Bad Behavior — an arbitrary shell command chosen by the model, with the operator's full environment, without confirmation or isolation.
policy_rule_text: Because this capability can read, modify, or destroy any file the operator can and can reach the network, it MUST run isolated from the host or require operator approval before executing a state-changing or destructive command, and it MUST NOT silently inherit the operator's full secret-bearing environment.

evidence.snippet: env = os.environ.copy(); then env updated with model-supplied env, passed to Popen — full operator environment inherited by the model's command.
subprocess.Popen(args, ...) runs the model command string (cmd /c, or powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Command) with no approval gate or allow/deny policy.

recommended_actions: Interpose a code-enforced approval gate on run_shell that blocks execution of state-changing/destructive commands until the operator confirms, and run the command under a restricted environment (drop secret-bearing vars) rather than os.environ.copy().
Add a per-command policy (allow/deny list) evaluated in code before Popen, independent of any model or prompt instruction.

findings.summary: The action executor runs model-authored Python via in-process exec() or a non-isolating venv subprocess, and pip-installs arbitrary declared requirements.
findings.description: In internal mode the executor calls exec(action_code) directly in the agent process; in sandboxed mode it runs the code in a persistent venv subprocess that shares the host filesystem, network, user, and inherited environment — the code comment states venv isolation is the sandbox, which provides no OS-level containment. It also pip-installs arbitrary package names from the action's requirements field. Model-generated code therefore executes with full host privileges, and the remit's requirement that code execution run isolated from the host is unmet by both modes.
policy_rule_text: Because this capability can read, modify, or destroy any file the operator can and can reach the network, it MUST run isolated from the host or require operator approval before executing a state-changing or destructive command, and it MUST NOT silently inherit the operator's full secret-bearing environment.

evidence.snippet: exec(action_code, local_ns, local_ns) runs model-authored code in-process (also :617 async path); no RestrictedPython, no seccomp, no container.
"_SANDBOX_BASE_PACKAGES = [] # empty - venv isolation is the sandbox" — sandboxed mode is a bare venv subprocess sharing host FS/network/env, not an isolation boundary.

recommended_actions: Execute model-authored action code inside a real isolation boundary (container, gVisor/seccomp, or a locked-down RestrictedPython interpreter) with an explicit minimal environment, not a venv subprocess inheriting os.environ.
Gate arbitrary pip installation from the requirements field behind an operator-approved allowlist rather than installing any declared package name.

findings.summary: Untrusted external content reaches ungated code/shell execution in one hop, autonomously, with no runtime input trust boundary or approval gate.
findings.description: Web fetches, search results, inbound messages, and imported content enter the LLM context with no sanitization (the app/security sanitizer is dead code), and the scheduler runs an autonomous heartbeat every 30 minutes with web_research and proactive action sets that can invoke run_shell and the exec engine. An attacker who plants instructions in a page the agent fetches can drive command or code execution with no human in the loop — the remit's escalation rule to halt when external content attempts a high-impact action is not enforced anywhere in code. This is the canonical indirect-injection-to-RCE chain, exploitable during unattended proactive runs.
policy_rule_text: Any attempt by external/untrusted content to drive a high-impact action.

evidence.snippet: heartbeat schedule "0,30 * * * *" runs enabled/recurring with action_sets [file_operations, proactive, web_research] — autonomous every-30-min loop that can reach run_shell and the exec engine.

web_fetch/web_search retrieve external page content into context with no trust labeling; sanitizer at app/security/prompt_sanitizer.py is never imported (see PRAX-005), so injected instructions reach the model unfiltered.

recommended_actions: Insert a code-enforced trust boundary between untrusted content (web/message/imported) and any execution action: block run_shell and the exec engine on turns whose plan derives from untrusted content, or require operator approval for those actions.
Enforce the remit's halt-and-alert escalation in code when retrieved/inbound content attempts a high-impact action, rather than relying on prompt instructions.

findings.summary: The agent can rewrite its own session-loaded identity files and chromadb memory with no code confirmation gate, enabling cross-session persistence from a single injection.
findings.description: SOUL.md (injected into the system prompt) and the AGENT.md operational manual expose a Self-Edit path, and the general write_file/stream_edit actions can overwrite files under agent_file_system/ while the memory pipeline distils events into a chromadb vector store and MEMORY.md — all reachable by the same untrusted content that drives PRAX-003, and the MUST-NOT-edit rule is prompt-only. Because these files and the vector memory feed every future session, one injected instruction can poison the agent's identity and retrieved memory persistently and invisibly, violating the remit's prohibition on editing identity/memory without operator confirmation.
policy_rule_text: Editing the operator-owned identity/policy files (SOUL.md, USER.md, AGENT.md) or the long-term memory store without operator confirmation.

evidence.snippet: "edit AGENT/USER/SOUL.md → ## Self-Edit" — the operational manual grants a self-edit path over the session-loaded identity files; the MEMORY.md "DO NOT EDIT" rule (AGENT.md:861) is prompt-only with no code gate.
memory pipeline distils events/outputs into a chromadb vector store and MEMORY.md consumed by the memory_search RAG action; the agent writes its own context back into the store with no validation.

recommended_actions: Make agent_file_system/ identity files (SOUL.md, USER.md, AGENT.md) and the long-term memory store require an explicit operator confirmation in code before any write, rather than a prompt-level DO NOT EDIT rule.

Validate and label content ingested into the chromadb memory store, and separate agent-generated/external-origin material from operator-authored memory so injected text cannot silently alter future retrieval.

findings.summary: The app/security prompt sanitizer and secure error handler exist in the repo but are never imported, so no input validation or output filtering runs.
findings.description: PromptSanitizer and SecureErrorHandler are defined but have zero consumers anywhere in app/, agent_core/, or craftos_integrations/ — the sanitizer file even labels itself example usage. The sanitizer would only truncate and log suspicious input (it never blocks), and neither module is wired into prompt construction or error handling, so the agent runs with no runtime input-trust boundary or output redaction. This is a declared-but-never-consulted control: the primitive exists but does not lift the posture because it is not on the execution path.

evidence.snippet: "def example_safe_routing_prompt(...)" — the only call path is an in-file example; grep for PromptSanitizer/sanitize_user_message across app, agent_core, craftos_integrations returns zero external consumers.
SecureErrorHandler defined but never imported anywhere outside its own file; error responses are not routed through it.

recommended_actions: Wire PromptSanitizer into the prompt-construction path so external content is labeled/validated (and make it reject, not merely log, injection patterns), or remove the module so it does not imply a control that is not present.
Route agent error responses through SecureErrorHandler (or an equivalent) so tracebacks and sensitive paths are not surfaced to users.

findings.summary: High-impact irreversible actions (sends, posts, payments, third-party writes) have no code-enforced approval gate; the irreversible flag drives only logging and idempotency.
findings.description: Actions such as send_message, integration writes, and payments are decorated irreversible=True, but that flag is consumed only to record intent to the activity ledger and to deduplicate — not to block execution pending approval. The confirm-with-the-user language lives in prompt text handed to the model (triggers/service.py, scheduler/manager.py), so whether a high-impact action is gated is left to model discretion. This directly violates the remit clause that agent-initiated high-impact actions require approval enforced by code, not model discretion alone.
policy_rule_text: Sending mail or posting publicly is a high-impact outbound action.

Any irreversible or externally-visible action: sending email/messages, posting publicly, making payments or other financial operations, and creating/modifying/deleting data in a connected third-party account.
an agent-initiated high-impact action requires explicit operator approval, and this gating MUST be enforced by code, not left to model discretion alone.

evidence.snippet: irreversible=True records intent to the activity ledger before the action — a log/idempotency step, not a blocking approval gate; no code path halts execution pending operator confirmation.

"time-sensitive or irreversible (e.g. sending a message or email), confirm with the user before proceeding" — confirmation is prompt guidance to the model (also triggers/service.py:340), not a deterministic code gate.

recommended_actions: Add a code-enforced approval gate keyed on the irreversible flag that blocks send/post/payment/third-party-write actions until the operator explicitly approves, independent of model output.
Treat the activity ledger as the audit record behind that gate rather than as the control itself.

findings.summary: Inbound messaging auto_reply defaults to true with no code-enforced sender-identity check, so an unknown sender can be answered as if they were the operator.
findings.description: external_comms_config.json ships auto_reply=true for both Telegram and WhatsApp, and the auto_reply flag has no consumer in the Python codebase — the decision to answer or act on an inbound message is left to model discretion via prompt instructions rather than a code check that the sender is the operator. The remit requires that inbound messages never be treated as authenticated operator commands without a sender-identity check; here there is no such gate, so a spoofed or unknown sender can steer the agent through a connected channel.
policy_rule_text: Inbound messages from a messaging channel must never be treated as authenticated operator commands without a sender-identity check.

evidence.snippet: telegram "auto_reply": true (whatsapp likewise at :18) — auto-reply enabled by default configuration.

grep for auto_reply across app/, agent_core/, craftos_integrations/ finds only the config file and the template AGENT.md — no code reads it, so no sender-identity gate enforces who may be auto-answered.

recommended_actions: Enforce a code-level sender-identity check against an operator allowlist before any inbound message is treated as an actionable command or auto-answered, on every messaging integration.
Default auto_reply to false and require the operator to designate which senders (if any) may be answered automatically.

findings.summary: The local control-plane WebSocket has no origin check and no authentication, so any local process or a malicious web page can drive the agent.
findings.description: The browser control plane binds localhost:7926 and its WebSocket handler prepares the connection without validating the Origin header or requiring an auth token. Because browsers permit cross-origin WebSocket connections, a website the operator visits (or a DNS-rebinding attacker) can open ws://localhost:7926 and issue control messages — starting tasks, connecting integrations, running actions — that reach the same ungated execution surfaces. This exposes the control surface to an unauthenticated caller, which the remit explicitly forbids, and undercuts the local-machine-only intent for the operator interface.
policy_rule_text: Exposing its control surface (task execution, settings, files, credentials) to the public internet or to other users.
Any capability that exposes agent control, files, or credentials to a network-reachable, unauthenticated caller.

evidence.snippet: _websocket_handler prepares web.WebSocketResponse and accepts messages with no Origin validation and no auth token check before dispatching control messages.
host="localhost" port 7926 (BROWSER_PORT override) — loopback bind is the only access control; no per-connection authentication layer exists on the control plane.

recommended_actions: Validate the Origin header against an allowlist and require a per-session authentication token on the control-plane WebSocket before accepting any control message.
Reject cross-origin WebSocket handshakes and bind strictly to loopback, so a visited web page cannot drive the agent via DNS rebinding.

findings.summary: The Living UI sidecar proxy and generated Living UI backends bind 0.0.0.0 with wildcard CORS, exposing agent-built and imported third-party apps to the local network.
findings.description: The Living UI sidecar proxy runs uvicorn on host 0.0.0.0 with CORS allow_origins ["*"], and the Living UI manager spawns generated project backends with --host 0.0.0.0 (the template backend does the same). These apps — including imported/marketplace projects that run third-party code — are therefore reachable from any host on the operator's network rather than loopback-only, and the wildcard CORS removes browser-side origin protection. This widens the exposure the remit intends to keep local and conflicts with the requirement that imported projects be isolated.
policy_rule_text: These MUST be isolated from the operator's credentials and broader filesystem and MUST come from a source the operator approved.

evidence.snippet: uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=args.proxy_port) with CORSMiddleware allow_origins=["*"] at :126 — sidecar (and the app behind it) bound to all interfaces.
generated Living UI backends spawned with uvicorn --host 0.0.0.0 (also :1965; template backend/main.py:137) — third-party project code reachable on the LAN.
template ships CORSMiddleware allow_origins=["*"], so every generated Living UI app inherits wildcard CORS by default.

recommended_actions: Bind the Living UI sidecar and generated backends to 127.0.0.1 and replace wildcard CORS with an explicit loopback origin allowlist.
Run imported/marketplace Living UI projects under filesystem and credential isolation, per the remit's isolation requirement for third-party code.

findings.summary: Operator OAuth and bot tokens are stored as plaintext JSON under .credentials/ rather than in an OS keychain or encrypted store.
findings.description: credentials_store.py writes each integration's secrets as plaintext JSON (e.g. github.json, slack.json) under the project's .credentials/ directory. The directory is chmod 0700 and files chmod 0600 and the path is git-ignored, which prevents world-readable and version-controlled exposure, but the tokens are still cleartext at rest — readable by any process running as the operator and by the default-enabled filesystem MCP server rooted at the project directory. The remit requires secrets be stored encrypted at rest (OS keychain or encrypted store), which is not met.
policy_rule_text: Secrets MUST be stored protected at rest (OS keychain or encrypted store), not as plaintext files, and MUST be excluded from any data the agent transmits.

evidence.snippet: _save_dataclass writes asdict(obj) as plaintext JSON to .credentials/<file>; chmod 0600 is applied but the token value is not encrypted.
_credentials_dir() = project_root/.credentials (chmod 0700); the default-enabled filesystem MCP server is rooted at "." and can read this directory.

recommended_actions: Store integration secrets in the OS keychain (Keychain/DPAPI/Secret Service) or an encrypted keystore, keeping only a reference in .credentials/.
Exclude the .credentials/ path from the filesystem MCP server's allowed root so third-party MCP code cannot read cleartext tokens.

findings.summary: 157 MCP servers ship bundled and are installed unpinned via npx -y / uvx on enable, with no provenance vetting and inheriting the full parent environment.
findings.description: mcp_config.json defines 157 third-party MCP servers (including kali, shodan, aws-s3, kubernetes, cloudflare, stripe, and exchange integrations); each is launched by fetching the package fresh through npx -y / uvx with no version pin, no integrity check, and no operator vetting step, and the MCP subprocess is started with env = os.environ.copy() so every server inherits the operator's full environment. The filesystem server is enabled by default rooted at the project directory. The remit requires MCP servers to come from an operator-approved source and be isolated from the operator's credentials and filesystem; neither pinning/vetting nor credential isolation is present.
policy_rule_text: These MUST be isolated from the operator's credentials and broader filesystem and MUST come from a source the operator approved.

evidence.snippet: 157 server definitions launched via unpinned npx -y / uvx / node commands; filesystem server enabled with args ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "."] rooted at the project dir.
full_env = os.environ.copy(); full_env.update(self.env) — the MCP subprocess inherits the operator's entire environment, defeating credential isolation for third-party server code.

recommended_actions: Pin MCP server package versions and add an operator approval/vetting step before a server may be enabled or first launched, instead of fetching @latest via npx -y.
Launch MCP subprocesses with a minimal explicit environment (only the keys that server needs) rather than os.environ.copy(), and scope the filesystem server away from .credentials and secret paths.

findings.summary: Python dependencies are largely unpinned with no committed lockfile, exposing the runtime to version-swap and dependency-confusion risk.
findings.description: requirements.txt lists roughly fifty dependencies, most with no version constraint at all and a handful floor-pinned with >= (openai, anthropic, google-genai, boto3, and similar); no requirements lock or poetry/pip-tools lockfile is committed. Installs therefore resolve to whatever versions are current, so a compromised or maliciously republished package version can enter the agent's trusted runtime with no pinned baseline to detect the change. This is a standard supply-chain hygiene gap the remit is silent on but the RAISE supply-chain category flags.

evidence.snippet: most entries (requests, pyyaml, chromadb, langgraph, playwright, pillow, lxml, ...) carry no version pin; only a few use >= floors; no == pins and no committed lockfile.

recommended_actions: Pin dependencies to exact versions and commit a lockfile (pip-tools/poetry) so the runtime is reproducible and version swaps are detectable.
Add a dependency-integrity/audit step to the build to catch known-vulnerable or unexpectedly-changed packages.

positives.title: LLM-call rate limiter wired into the model path
positives.description: A token-budget rate limiter is actually consumed on the LLM request path (wait_if_needed / record_usage), providing a real denial-of-wallet / unbounded-consumption mitigation rather than an unused config knob.

positives.title: Irreversible-action activity ledger with idempotency
positives.description: Irreversible side effects are recorded to a deterministic-keyed SQLite ledger before execution, giving a durable audit trail and preventing duplicate sends/payments on retry.

positives.title: Credential files git-ignored and permission-restricted
positives.description: The .credentials/ directory is excluded from version control and created chmod 0700 with files chmod 0600, so tokens are neither committed nor world-readable even though they remain plaintext.

positives.title: MCP servers disabled by default
positives.description: Of the 157 bundled MCP server definitions, 156 ship disabled; only filesystem and playwright are enabled, limiting the default third-party-code attack surface.

rows.content_type: plaintext log lines (timestamp | level | agent | location | message)
rows.purpose: main-agent and framework/startup activity for one process run
full interleaved timeline across the main agent and every sub-agent
Living UI sidecar and injected browser console/network capture

rows.content_type: SQLite rows keyed by deterministic side-effect identity
rows.purpose: audit and idempotency record of irreversible actions (sends, payments, destructive writes)

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent. The framework-weighted posture is dragged to the floor by a Zero-Trust score of 0: CraftBot's core execution model is ungated, un-isolated code and shell execution driven by model output, with the only "controls" being prompt-tier approval language and an app/security module that is never invoked. The surrounding categories are ad hoc — general-purpose scope with prompt-only limits, external content entering context unsanitized, unpinned dependencies and 157 unvetted MCP servers, and no adversarial-testing evidence. The one bright spot is monitoring: durable rotating logs and an irreversible-action activity ledger give partial after-the-fact visibility, but they observe rather than prevent, so they do not lift the overall posture out of the ungated-execution hole.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: CraftBot is general-purpose by design, but domain restriction is prompt-only (SOUL.md/AGENT.md) with no code gate, and the tool inventory — a direct shell action, in-process code execution, all operator integrations, and 157 bundled MCP servers — vastly exceeds any single task's Known Good Baseline with no per-task scoping.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: External content (web_fetch/web_search, inbound messages, imported Living UI) enters the LLM context unsanitized — the app/security prompt sanitizer is dead code — and the memory pipeline distils the agent's own events and outputs into a chromadb vector store and MEMORY.md with no validation, so the knowledge base is both unfiltered on ingest and self-poisoning.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: There is no code-level interposition on the agent's consequential actions: run_shell executes model commands with os.environ.copy() and no approval, executor.py runs model code in-process or in a non-isolating venv, the irreversible-action flag drives only logging/idempotency (confirmation is model-discretion prompt text), and the WebSocket control plane has no origin check or authentication.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: requirements.txt is largely unpinned with no committed lockfile, and app/config/mcp_config.json bundles 157 MCP servers installed unpinned via npx -y / uvx on enable with no provenance or vetting and inheriting the full parent environment; credentials are stored plaintext (though git-ignored and chmod-restricted).

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The tests/ directory holds only functional and unit tests (triggers, event stream, token attribution) with no adversarial, prompt-injection, or red-team artifacts, and the abandoned app/security module suggests security was considered but never exercised against the running agent.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: app/logger.py provides durable per-run rotating logs (main.log/all.log/per-subagent) with timestamp, level, and agent attribution, and app/triggers/activity_log.py keeps a SQLite ledger of irreversible actions, but log content is free-form rather than a structured security-event schema and there is no alerting on high-impact actions.
